techniques.evidence_quote: It's our working theory that the AWS access keys for the arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/comp_user2 account were discovered by the attacker in a publicly available code repository.
the attacker first issued a ListUsers API call using the arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/comp_user2 IAM user from the AWS command line interface (aws-cli).
The CreateAccessKey API call creates a new long-term access key for a specific user.

the attacker issued the following API calls: CreateUser, CreateAccessKey, AttachUserPolicy, CreateLoginProfile. This allowed the attacker to create two new IAM users with accompanying long-term access keys

the attacker issued an API call to UpdateLoginProfile to change the AWS console password for the arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/comp_user1 account. Authenticated into the AWS console using the arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/comp_user1 account.

AttachUserPolicy attaches a specified policy to a specified user... the attacker issued an AttachUserPolicy API call to attach an AdministratorAccess policy to the newly created IAM user — allowing the attacker to elevate their privileges in AWS.
the attacker used one of the newly created IAM users to make a call to the RequestServiceQuotaIncrease API
It's our opinion that this action was taken in preparation for starting multiple large EC2 instances for cryptocurrency mining.

attack_chain.description: Attacker discovered long-term IAM access keys (AKIA...) for comp_user2 in a publicly available code repository, obtaining credentials without any exploitation.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: It's our working theory that the AWS access keys for the arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/comp_user2 account were discovered by the attacker in a publicly available code repository.

attack_chain.description: Using the stolen comp_user2 access keys via the AWS CLI, the attacker called ListUsers to enumerate IAM users in the account, identifying comp_user1 as a target.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker first issued a ListUsers API call using the arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/comp_user2 IAM user from the AWS command line interface (aws-cli).

attack_chain.description: The attacker called UpdateLoginProfile via the CLI to set/change the AWS console password for comp_user1 (succeeded), enabling console login for an account that may not have had one. The same call for comp_user2 failed.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker issued two calls to the UpdateLoginProfile API; one for the arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/comp_user1 IAM user (succeeded) and one for the arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/comp_user2 IAM user (failed with the NoSuchEntityException reason).

attack_chain.description: Attacker authenticated to the AWS Management Console as comp_user1 from an IP address in Indonesia, with no MFA, using the password set in the previous step.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The authentication originated from Indonesia... CloudTrail logs recorded a ConsoleLogin from the arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/comp_user1 account.

attack_chain.description: Immediately after console login, the attacker called CreateUser twice to create two new backdoor IAM user accounts (created_user1 and created_user2).
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Immediately following the authentication to the AWS console, the attacker issued the following API calls: CreateUser, CreateAccessKey, AttachUserPolicy, CreateLoginProfile. This allowed the attacker to create two new IAM users

attack_chain.description: The attacker called CreateAccessKey for the newly created IAM users, generating long-term access keys (AKIA...) to enable persistent programmatic access independent of the console session.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The CreateAccessKey API call creates a new long-term access key for a specific user... create two new IAM users with accompanying long-term access keys

attack_chain.description: The attacker called AttachUserPolicy to attach the AWS-managed AdministratorAccess policy to one of the newly created IAM users, granting full administrative privileges.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker issued an AttachUserPolicy API call to attach an AdministratorAccess policy to the newly created IAM user — allowing the attacker to elevate their privileges in AWS.

attack_chain.description: The attacker also called CreateLoginProfile for one of the new IAM users to set a console password, further entrenching console access persistence.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: attached a policy that allowed one of the new users to create and change their AWS console password

attack_chain.description: Using one of the newly created IAM users, the attacker called RequestServiceQuotaIncrease to raise the EC2 instance quota in preparation for launching large EC2 instances for cryptocurrency mining. Expel intervened before mining began.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker used one of the newly created IAM users to make a call to the RequestServiceQuotaIncrease API in order to increase the EC2 quota. It's our opinion that this action was taken in preparation for starting multiple large EC2 instances for cryptocurrency mining.

initial_access_vector: Long-term IAM access keys (AKIA) for comp_user2 were exposed in a publicly available code repository and obtained by the attacker
persistence_mechanism: Attacker created two new IAM users (created_user1, created_user2) with long-term access keys and console passwords; one user had AdministratorAccess policy attached
privilege_escalation: AttachUserPolicy API call used to attach the AWS-managed AdministratorAccess policy to a newly created IAM user, escalating from limited comp_user2 privileges to full admin
